CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): Overall the paper is reasonably well organized (Intro → Related Work → RLE design → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early in the introduction (three bullet points). Notation is mostly defined (e.g., the Q-learning update and symbols α, γ, θ) but the equation formatting is cluttered and some terms are described awkwardly. Figures/tables are frequently referenced (e.g., “Figure (1)”, “Table (3)”) but not shown here and the text contains repeated and garbled paragraphs and grammatical/sloppy phrasing (e.g., repeated sentences near the end), which reduce clarity and polish. | B(3): Overall the paper is organized in a standard, logical order (Introduction → Related Work → RLE/Implementation → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are clearly listed early in the Introduction. However, the presentation suffers from inconsistent and sometimes broken notation/formatting (Equation (1) is poorly typeset and symbols are unclear), many missing figure/table references (e.g. Figure (), Table (1)/(3) are cited but not shown), and repeated/duplicated text blocks toward the end. The writing has frequent typos and grammatical slips (e.g. misuse of "it’s" vs "its"), which together with the formatting issues make some sections hard to follow despite adequate overall structure.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is reasonably well organized (Intro → Related Work → RLE design → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early in the introduction (three bullet points). Notation is mostly defined (e.g., the Q-learning update and symbols α, γ, θ) but the equation formatting is cluttered and some terms are described awkwardly. Figures/tables are frequently referenced (e.g., “Figure (1)”, “Table (3)”) but not shown here and the text contains repeated and garbled paragraphs and grammatical/sloppy phrasing (e.g., repeated sentences near the end), which reduce clarity and polish.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is organized in a standard, logical order (Introduction → Related Work → RLE/Implementation → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are clearly listed early in the Introduction. However, the presentation suffers from inconsistent and sometimes broken notation/formatting (Equation (1) is poorly typeset and symbols are unclear), many missing figure/table references (e.g. Figure (), Table (1)/(3) are cited but not shown), and repeated/duplicated text blocks toward the end. The writing has frequent typos and grammatical slips (e.g. misuse of "it’s" vs "its"), which together with the formatting issues make some sections hard to follow despite adequate overall structure.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces the Retro Learning Environment (RLE), a new RL benchmark that exposes many more and more complex consoles (SNES/Genesis and potentially 7,000+ games via LibRetro) and adds native multi-agent/ensemble-training support—distinguishing it from ALE and from OpenAI Universe (which uses remote VNC). The distinctions from prior work are explicitly discussed (Section 2) and experiments show novel uses (multi-agent training and opponent-ensemble robustness). However, the contribution is primarily an engineered environment/platform rather than new learning algorithms, so it is a notable but not radical methodological advance. | B(4): The paper presents a new RL environment (RLE) that extends ALE-style benchmarking to SNES/Genesis and many other consoles via LibRetro, enabling access to thousands of additional games (sections 3.2–3.4) — a substantive platform contribution distinct from prior work (it contrasts with ALE and OpenAI Universe’s VNC approach, sec. 2.1 and 2.4). It also adds multi-agent evaluation features and an alternating-opponent training protocol shown to improve robustness (sec. 4.3). While primarily an engineering/integration effort rather than a novel algorithm, the work clearly differentiates itself from prior environments and introduces capabilities that would meaningfully broaden RL evaluation, so its novelty is notable rather than merely incremental.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces the Retro Learning Environment (RLE), a new RL benchmark that exposes many more and more complex consoles (SNES/Genesis and potentially 7,000+ games via LibRetro) and adds native multi-agent/ensemble-training support—distinguishing it from ALE and from OpenAI Universe (which uses remote VNC). The distinctions from prior work are explicitly discussed (Section 2) and experiments show novel uses (multi-agent training and opponent-ensemble robustness). However, the contribution is primarily an engineered environment/platform rather than new learning algorithms, so it is a notable but not radical methodological advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a new RL environment (RLE) that extends ALE-style benchmarking to SNES/Genesis and many other consoles via LibRetro, enabling access to thousands of additional games (sections 3.2–3.4) — a substantive platform contribution distinct from prior work (it contrasts with ALE and OpenAI Universe’s VNC approach, sec. 2.1 and 2.4). It also adds multi-agent evaluation features and an alternating-opponent training protocol shown to improve robustness (sec. 4.3). While primarily an engineering/integration effort rather than a novel algorithm, the work clearly differentiates itself from prior environments and introduces capabilities that would meaningfully broaden RL evaluation, so its novelty is notable rather than merely incremental.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper clearly states its primary claim (introducing RLE) and provides implementation details and released code, and the DQN update equation shown is standard and correct. However the experimental support is incomplete: results are referenced to missing figures/tables (e.g. Table (3), Figure references), hyperparameters, seeds and training budgets are underspecified, and evaluation choices are problematic (epsilon-greedy kept during testing, random-score subtraction, no statistical tests or variance reported). The case studies (reward shaping, multi-agent ensemble) show plausible effects but lack controls and rigorous quantitative analysis, so the conclusions are only partially supported by the presented evidence. | B(3): The paper’s main claims (introducing RLE, showing SNES is more challenging than Atari, demonstrating reward shaping and basic MARL use) are stated and supported by implementation and experimental examples (sections 3 and 4), and the presented Q‑learning update is standard/correct. However the experimental methodology has notable omissions: no detailed hyperparameters/seeds, no description of which action‑subsets were used, no statistical tests or error bars, and they even keep ϵ‑greedy exploration during testing (Sec. 4.1), which can bias evaluation. These gaps and some unsupported assumptions (e.g. that 84×84 greyscale preserves all relevant information) weaken the empirical support, so the technical claims are only partially validated.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its primary claim (introducing RLE) and provides implementation details and released code, and the DQN update equation shown is standard and correct. However the experimental support is incomplete: results are referenced to missing figures/tables (e.g. Table (3), Figure references), hyperparameters, seeds and training budgets are underspecified, and evaluation choices are problematic (epsilon-greedy kept during testing, random-score subtraction, no statistical tests or variance reported). The case studies (reward shaping, multi-agent ensemble) show plausible effects but lack controls and rigorous quantitative analysis, so the conclusions are only partially supported by the presented evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims (introducing RLE, showing SNES is more challenging than Atari, demonstrating reward shaping and basic MARL use) are stated and supported by implementation and experimental examples (sections 3 and 4), and the presented Q‑learning update is standard/correct. However the experimental methodology has notable omissions: no detailed hyperparameters/seeds, no description of which action‑subsets were used, no statistical tests or error bars, and they even keep ϵ‑greedy exploration during testing (Sec. 4.1), which can bias evaluation. These gaps and some unsupported assumptions (e.g. that 84×84 greyscale preserves all relevant information) weaken the empirical support, so the technical claims are only partially validated.
